preamble.summary: Psalm 49 is a wisdom psalm of the Sons of Korah that confronts the mystery of death's equality — the rich and the poor, the wise and the foolish, all die. The psalm opens with a universal summons to hear wisdom, then exposes the folly of trusting in wealth: no amount of money can ransom a person from death. The rich and the foolish perish alike, their graves become their permanent homes, and their wealth passes to others. A key refrain declares that humanity in its pomp will not endure — like the beasts, they perish. Yet the psalmist expresses a singular hope: 'God will ransom my soul from the hand of Sheol, for he will take me.'
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 49 is the Hebrew Bible's most sustained meditation on the inability of wealth to defeat death, making it a biblical counterpart to Ecclesiastes. The refrain adam biqar ve-lo yavin nimshal ka-behemot nidmu ('humanity in its splendor without understanding is like the beasts that perish,' vv. 13, 21) reduces the wealthy to animals — all their glory cannot make them more than creatures who die and decompose. But verse 16 breaks through the psalm's bleak equality with a startling hope: akh Elohim yifdeh nafshi mi-yad She'ol ki yiqqacheni ('but God will ransom my soul from the hand of Sheol, for he will take me'). The verb laqach ('to take') is the same used for Enoch (Genesis 5:24, 'God took him') and Elijah (2 Kings 2:3, 'the LORD will take your master'). This is the Hebrew Bible's most explicit assertion of a hope beyond death for the righteous individual — not merely prolonged life, but rescue from Sheol itself.
preamble.friction: The text of Psalm 49 is notoriously difficult, with several verses that resist easy translation. The refrain varies slightly between verses 13 and 21 (yalin vs. yavin — 'will not abide' vs. 'does not understand'), and scholars debate which is original and which a corruption. Verse 15 (Hebrew) is extremely compressed: qirbam battemo le-olam ('their inward thought is that their houses are forever') reconstructs what may be a corrupted text. The hope of verse 16 is the psalm's theological problem: how can the psalmist claim rescue from Sheol when the rest of the psalm insists that all die? The answer may lie in the distinction between death (which all share) and Sheol's power (from which God can ransom).
preamble.connections: The wisdom framework connects to Proverbs, Job, and Ecclesiastes, all of which address wealth's inability to secure life. The verb laqach in verse 16 ('he will take me') links to the Enoch tradition (Genesis 5:24) and the Elijah tradition (2 Kings 2:10). Psalm 73:23-26 develops the same hope: 'You guide me with your counsel, and afterward you will take me into glory.' Jesus' parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) and his teaching that a person cannot serve God and money (Matthew 6:24) echo this psalm's central argument. The refrain's comparison of wealthy humans to perishing beasts inverts Genesis 1:26-28, where humanity is given dominion over the animals — here, without wisdom, humans become indistinguishable from them.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֗חַ לִבְנֵי־קֹ֥רַח מִזְמֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm for the sons of Korah.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. A psalm of the Sons of Korah.
translator_notes: Verse 1 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁמְעוּ־זֹ֥את כׇּל־הָעַמִּ֑ים הַ֝אֲזִ֗ינוּ כׇּל־יֹשְׁבֵ֥י חָֽלֶד׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear this, all ye people; give ear, all ye inhabitants of the world:
verses.rendering:
Hear this, all you peoples;
give ear, all who inhabit the passing world.
translator_notes: The summons is universal: kol ha-ammim ('all the peoples') and kol yoshve chaled ('all who inhabit chaled'). The word chaled ('world, age, duration') is rare and emphasizes the transitory nature of earthly existence — from a root meaning 'to glide, to pass away.' The world itself is described by a word that means 'the fleeting thing.' The psalm addresses all of humanity, not just Israel.

key_terms.semantic_range: world, age, duration, fleeting time, transient existence
key_terms.note: chaled emphasizes the transitory quality of earthly existence. Unlike olam ('eternity') or tevel ('inhabited world'), chaled foregrounds the temporary nature of the world and the life lived in it.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־בְּנֵ֣י אָדָ֣ם גַּם־בְּנֵי־אִ֑ישׁ יַ֝֗חַד עָשִׁ֥יר וְאֶבְיֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: Both low and high, rich and poor, together.
verses.rendering:
Both common folk and the highborn,
rich and poor together.
translator_notes: bene adam ('sons of Adam/humanity') refers to ordinary people — the common person. bene ish ('sons of a man/person of standing') refers to those of higher social rank. The pairing covers the entire social spectrum, then further specifies: ashir ve-evyon ('rich and destitute'). The psalm addresses everyone without exception — its wisdom applies regardless of status or wealth.

verses.text_hebrew: פִּ֣י יְ֭דַבֵּר חׇכְמ֑וֹת וְהָג֥וּת לִ֝בִּ֗י תְּבוּנֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: My mouth shall speak of wisdom; and the meditation of my heart shall be of understanding.
verses.rendering:
My mouth will speak wisdom,
and the meditation of my heart will be understanding.
translator_notes: khokhmot ('wisdoms,' plural) — the plural may indicate comprehensive wisdom, wisdom in its full range. hagut libbi ('the meditation/murmuring of my heart') uses hagah, the same verb from Psalm 1:2 for meditating on Torah. tevunot ('understandings,' plural) parallels the plural of wisdom. The psalmist claims prophetic and sapiential authority: what follows is not opinion but divinely informed wisdom.

verses.text_hebrew: אַטֶּ֣ה לְמָשָׁ֣ל אׇזְנִ֑י אֶפְתַּ֥ח בְּ֝כִנּ֗וֹר חִידָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I will incline mine ear to a parable: I will open my dark saying upon the harp.
verses.rendering:
I will turn my ear to a proverb;
I will open my riddle on the lyre.
verses.expanded_rendering: The sage listens before speaking: atteh le-mashal ozni ('I will incline my ear to a mashal') — mashal ('proverb, comparison, parable') is the basic unit of wisdom literature. The sage first receives the teaching, then transmits it. eftach be-khinnor chidati ('I will open my riddle on the lyre') — the chidah ('riddle, enigma, dark saying') is the puzzle that wisdom addresses: why do the wicked prosper? Why does wealth fail? The lyre (kinnor) indicates that this wisdom is sung, not merely spoken — the truth is set to music for deeper penetration.
translator_notes: The combination of mashal ('proverb') and chidah ('riddle') echoes Proverbs 1:6, where both terms describe the content of wisdom. A chidah is not a puzzle for entertainment but a deep truth concealed in paradox — the kind of truth that requires meditation to unlock.

verses.text_hebrew: לָמָּ֣ה אִ֭ירָא בִּ֣ימֵי רָ֑ע עֲוֺ֖ן עֲקֵבַ֣י יְסוּבֵּֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Wherefore should I fear in the days of evil, when the iniquity of my heels shall compass me about?
verses.rendering:
Why should I fear in days of trouble,
when the guilt of those who track me surrounds me?
translator_notes: The verse is textually difficult. avon aqevai ('the iniquity of my heels/my supplanters') may mean: (1) the guilt of my own missteps, (2) the wickedness of those who pursue me (aqev as 'heel-tracker,' cf. Jacob's name), or (3) the sins that dog my footsteps. The context — a psalm about the futility of wealth — suggests the psalmist asks why he should fear when surrounded by the wickedness of the wealthy who oppress him.

verses.text_hebrew: הַבֹּטְחִ֥ים עַל־חֵילָ֑ם וּבְרֹ֥ב עׇ֝שְׁרָ֗ם יִתְהַלָּֽלוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They that trust in their wealth, and boast themselves in the multitude of their riches;
verses.rendering:
Those who trust in their wealth
and boast in the abundance of their riches —
translator_notes: ha-botchim al chelam ('those who trust in their resources') — the verb batach ('to trust') is the word the psalms reserve for confidence in God. Using it for wealth is a pointed accusation: they have placed in money the trust that belongs to God alone. u-ve-rov oshram yithallalu ('and in the greatness of their riches they boast') — the verb halal ('to boast, to praise') is also worship language. They praise their wealth as others praise God.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֗ח לֹא־פָדֹ֣ה יִפְדֶּ֣ה אִ֑ישׁ לֹא־יִתֵּ֖ן לֵאלֹהִ֣ים כׇּפְרֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: None of them can by any means redeem his brother, nor give to God a ransom for him:
verses.rendering:
No one can ransom a brother
or pay God the price of his release,
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's central theological statement begins. ach lo fadoh yifdeh ish ('a brother cannot redeem — no person can redeem') — the infinitive absolute fadoh before the finite yifdeh creates emphasis: absolutely cannot redeem. No human being, regardless of wealth, can buy another person's life from death. lo yitten le-Elohim kofro ('he cannot give to God his ransom-price') — kofer ('ransom, cover price') is the price paid to release a condemned person. In Exodus 21:30, a kofer can be paid for a life; here, the psalm insists that God will not accept any price. Death is not a debt that money can settle.
translator_notes: The verb padah ('to redeem, to ransom') is covenantal language — God ransoms (podeh) Israel from Egypt, from exile, from Sheol. The psalm's devastating point is that what humans cannot do for each other, God can do — setting up the hope of verse 16.

key_terms.semantic_range: ransom, cover-price, atonement payment, bribe, redemption price
key_terms.note: kofer is the price paid to redeem a life from a death sentence. It is related to kippur ('atonement, covering'). The psalm declares that no kofer can be paid to God for a human life — death cannot be bought off.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיֵקַ֥ר פִּדְי֥וֹן נַפְשָׁ֑ם וְחָדַ֥ל לְעוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: For the redemption of their soul is precious, and it ceaseth for ever:
verses.rendering:
for the ransom of a life is too costly —
he must let it go forever.
translator_notes: yeqar pidyon nafsham ('costly is the redemption of their soul/life') — yeqar means 'precious, expensive, rare.' The ransom price for a human life exceeds all possible payment. ve-chadal le-olam ('and he ceases forever') — the verb chadal ('to cease, to desist, to give up') means the would-be redeemer must abandon the attempt. No amount of wealth reaches the price of a life.

verses.text_hebrew: וִיחִי־ע֥וֹד לָנֶ֑צַח לֹ֖א יִרְאֶ֣ה הַשָּֽׁחַת׃
verses.text_kjv: That he should still live for ever, and not see corruption.
verses.rendering:
that he should live on forever
and never see the pit.
translator_notes: The purpose clause completes the thought: no one can pay enough to live forever (yichi od lanestsach) or to avoid the shachat ('pit, grave, corruption'). shachat can mean the physical grave or the decomposition of the body — death in its most unsparing form.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י יִרְאֶ֨ה ׀ חֲכָמִ֣ים יָמ֗וּתוּ יַ֤חַד כְּסִ֣יל וָבַ֣עַר יֹאבֵ֑דוּ וְעָזְב֖וּ לַאֲחֵרִ֣ים חֵילָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: For he seeth that wise men die, likewise the fool and the brutish person perish, and leave their wealth to others.
translator_notes: Death's equality is stated bluntly: chakhamim yamuthu ('the wise die'), and yachad kesil u-va'ar yovedu ('together the fool and the brute perish'). The three categories — wise, foolish, and brutish — cover the entire spectrum of human intelligence. None is exempt. ve-azvu la-acherim chelam ('and they leave their wealth to others') — the final insult. The wealth they spent their lives accumulating passes to people they may not have chosen.

verses.text_hebrew: קִרְבָּ֗ם בָּתֵּ֥ימוֹ לְעוֹלָ֗ם מִ֭שְׁכְּנֹתָם לְדֹ֣ר וָדֹ֑ר קָרְא֥וּ בִ֝שְׁמוֹתָ֗ם עֲלֵ֣י אֲדָמֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Their inward thought is, that their houses shall continue for ever, and their dwelling places to all generations; they call their lands after their own names.
verses.rendering:
Their graves are their homes forever,
their dwelling places for all generations,
though they named lands after themselves.
verses.expanded_rendering: The text is difficult but the meaning is devastating. qirbam battemo le-olam ('their grave is their home forever') — many scholars emend qirbam ('their inward part') to qivram ('their grave'), which fits the context: the tomb becomes the only permanent dwelling. mishkenotam le-dor va-dor ('their dwelling places from generation to generation') — the grave persists while the living houses crumble. qar'u vishmotam ale adamot ('they called by their names upon the lands') — they named estates, cities, and territories after themselves, seeking immortality through reputation. But the naming cannot prevent the grave from becoming their final and permanent address.
translator_notes: The emendation of qirbam ('their inner thought') to qivram ('their grave') is widely accepted, as the resh and bet are visually similar in Hebrew script. The MT reading ('their inner thought is that their houses will last forever') makes sense but is less poetically parallel. Either way, the irony is sharp: they planned permanence and received a tomb.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאָדָ֣ם בִּ֭יקָר בַּל־יָלִ֑ין נִמְשַׁ֖ל כַּבְּהֵמ֣וֹת נִדְמֽוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Nevertheless man being in honour abideth not: he is like the beasts that perish.
verses.rendering:
Humanity in its splendor will not endure;
it is like the beasts that perish.
verses.expanded_rendering: The first appearance of the psalm's haunting refrain. adam biqar bal yalin ('humanity in its preciousness/honor will not lodge/remain') — the verb lin ('to lodge, to spend the night') suggests that human glory is a single night's stay at an inn. You check in, you check out. biqar ('in preciousness, in splendor, in honor') acknowledges that human life has genuine value and beauty — but none of it lasts. nimshal ka-behemot nidmu ('he is compared to the beasts; they are silenced/destroyed') — the verb damah can mean 'to be like' or 'to be destroyed.' The double meaning is the refrain's genius: humans resemble animals in death, and like animals, they are destroyed. All the dignity of being made in God's image does not prevent the rich from dying like cattle.
translator_notes: The refrain in verse 21 will change one word: yalin ('abide') becomes yavin ('understand'). The shift is significant — the first refrain says wealth does not make you permanent; the second says wealth does not make you wise. Together they strip wealth of both its promises: durability and meaning.

key_terms.semantic_range: preciousness, honor, splendor, value, costliness, dignity
key_terms.note: yeqar ('precious, costly, rare') is used for jewels, for royal honor, and here for the splendor of human life. The psalm does not deny that human life is precious; it insists that preciousness does not confer permanence.

verses.text_hebrew: זֶ֣ה דַ֭רְכָּם כֵּ֣סֶל לָ֑מוֹ וְאַחֲרֵיהֶ֓ם ׀ בְּפִיהֶ֖ם יִרְצ֣וּ סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: This their way is their folly: yet their posterity approve their sayings. Selah.
verses.rendering:
This is their way — their foolish confidence;
and those who follow them approve their words. Selah.
translator_notes: kesel ('folly, false confidence, stupidity') is their fundamental error — trusting in wealth. ve-achareihem be-fihem yirtsu ('and those after them delight in their mouth/words') — subsequent generations repeat the same folly, approving the worldview of the rich who came before them. The cycle of foolish confidence reproduces itself.

verses.text_hebrew: כַּצֹּ֤אן ׀ לִשְׁא֣וֹל שַׁ֭תּוּ מָ֣וֶת יִרְעֵ֑ם וַיִּרְדּ֖וּ בָ֣ם יְשָׁרִ֤ים ׀ לַבֹּ֗קֶר וְ֭צוּרָם לְבַלּ֥וֹת שְׁא֗וֹל מִזְּבֻ֥ל לֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Like sheep they are laid in the grave; death shall feed on them; and the upright shall have dominion over them in the morning; and their beauty shall consume in the grave from their dwelling.
verses.rendering:
Like sheep they are driven to Sheol;
death is their shepherd.
The upright will rule over them at morning,
and their form will waste away —
Sheol will be their dwelling, not a mansion.
verses.expanded_rendering: The most vivid verse in the psalm. ka-tson li-She'ol shattu ('like sheep to Sheol they are appointed/driven') — the wealthy are herded to death like livestock going to slaughter. mavet yir'em ('death shepherds them') — the verb ra'ah ('to shepherd, to tend') creates a nightmarish inversion: instead of a shepherd leading sheep to green pastures (Psalm 23), death leads the rich to the underworld. vayyirdu vam yesharim labboqer ('and the upright will rule over them in the morning') — at dawn, which is the traditional hour of divine reversal, the righteous triumph. ve-tsuram leballot She'ol mi-zevul lo ('and their form will consume away; Sheol rather than a fine dwelling will be their home') — the body that was pampered in luxury will decompose in the underworld. Their zevul ('lofty dwelling, mansion') is replaced by Sheol.
translator_notes: The image of death as shepherd (mavet yir'em) is a deliberate inversion of Psalm 23 (YHVH ro'i, 'the LORD is my shepherd'). Those who refuse God as shepherd get death as shepherd instead. The 'morning' (boqer) of reversal echoes the dawn deliverance motif of Psalm 46:6.

verses.text_hebrew: אַךְ־אֱלֹהִ֗ים יִפְדֶּ֣ה נַ֭פְשִׁי מִיַּד־שְׁא֑וֹל כִּ֖י יִקָּחֵ֣נִי סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: But God will redeem my soul from the power of the grave: for he shall receive me. Selah.
verses.rendering:
But God will ransom my life from the hand of Sheol,
for he will take me. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's theological breakthrough. akh Elohim yifdeh nafshi mi-yad She'ol ('but God will ransom my soul from the hand of Sheol') — the adversative akh ('but, surely, however') marks the contrast with everything that precedes. No human can ransom another (v. 8), but God can ransom the psalmist. The verb padah ('to ransom, to redeem') is exactly the word declared impossible in verse 8 — what no human could do, God will do. mi-yad She'ol ('from the hand of Sheol') personifies the underworld as a grasping hand that holds the dead. God will pry that hand open. ki yiqqacheni ('for he will take me') — the verb laqach ('to take') is the Enoch verb. Genesis 5:24: 'Enoch walked with God, and he was not, for God took him (laqach).' 2 Kings 2:3: 'The LORD will take (laqach) your master today.' To be 'taken' by God is to be transferred from the realm of death to the realm of God's presence. This single verse stands as the Hebrew Bible's clearest hope for life beyond death.
translator_notes: The selah after this verse provides a moment of silence for the most radical claim in the psalm. After twelve verses insisting that all die, that no ransom suffices, that humans perish like beasts — this verse says: but not me. Not because of my wealth or wisdom, but because God ransoms. The basis is not human merit but divine action.

The echo of padah from verse 8 is deliberate. The psalm sets up an impossibility (no human can ransom) and then resolves it with divine capability (God can ransom). The structure mirrors the gospel pattern: what is impossible for humans is possible for God.

key_terms.semantic_range: to take, to receive, to fetch, to carry away, to seize
key_terms.note: laqach ('to take') in the context of divine action upon a righteous person signals transfer from earthly existence to God's presence. It is the Enoch verb (Genesis 5:24) and the Elijah verb (2 Kings 2:3). Its use here implies that the psalmist expects to be 'taken' by God in a manner that defeats Sheol's claim.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּ֭ירָא כִּֽי־יַעֲשִׁ֣ר אִ֑ישׁ כִּ֥י יִ֝רְבֶּ֗ה כְּב֣וֹד בֵּיתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Be not thou afraid when one is made rich, when the glory of his house is increased;
verses.rendering:
Do not fear when a man grows rich,
when the glory of his house increases,
translator_notes: The practical application of the psalm's wisdom: al tira ('do not fear') — do not be intimidated or envious when others accumulate wealth. kevod beto ('the glory of his house') — the impressive display of a wealthy household. The psalm's message: this glory is temporary and ultimately powerless against death.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֣א בְ֭מוֹתוֹ יִקַּ֣ח הַכֹּ֑ל לֹא־יֵרֵ֖ד אַחֲרָ֣יו כְּבוֹדֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: For when he dieth he shall carry nothing away: his glory shall not descend after him.
verses.rendering:
for when he dies he will take nothing with him;
his glory will not follow him down.
translator_notes: lo be-moto yiqqach hakkol ('not in his death will he take everything') — the verb laqach ('to take') reappears. In verse 16, God takes the psalmist; here, the rich man cannot take his possessions. The contrast is devastating: the righteous are taken by God; the wealthy cannot take their goods. lo yered acharav kevodo ('his glory will not descend after him') — kavod ('glory, weight, substance') stays above ground while the body descends to the grave.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־נַ֭פְשׁוֹ בְּחַיָּ֣יו יְבָרֵ֑ךְ וְ֝יוֹדֻ֗ךָ כִּי־תֵיטִ֥יב לָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Though while he lived he blessed his soul: and men will praise thee, when thou doest well to thyself.
verses.rendering:
Though he blesses himself during his life,
and people praise you when you prosper,
translator_notes: nafsho be-chayyav yevarekh ('he blesses his soul during his life') — self-congratulation, the rich man telling himself how well he has done. ve-yodukha ki tetiv lakh ('and they praise you when you do well for yourself') — others affirm and celebrate your success. The psalm exposes the feedback loop of wealth: prosperity generates self-praise and social approval, creating the illusion of permanence.

verses.text_hebrew: תָּב֗וֹא עַד־דּ֥וֹר אֲבוֹתָ֑יו עַד־נֵ֝֗צַח לֹ֣א יִרְאוּ־אֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall go to the generation of his fathers; they shall never see light.
verses.rendering:
he will go to the generation of his ancestors;
they will never see light.
translator_notes: tavo ad dor avotav ('he will come to the generation of his fathers') — he will join his dead ancestors in Sheol. ad netsach lo yir'u or ('unto perpetuity they will not see light') — the finality is total: the light that the psalm promised to the righteous (at morning, v. 15) is denied forever to those who trusted in wealth. The contrast with verse 16 is sharp: the psalmist will be taken by God; the wealthy will never see light.

verses.text_hebrew: אָדָ֣ם בִּ֭יקָר וְלֹ֣א יָבִ֑ין נִמְשַׁ֖ל כַּבְּהֵמ֣וֹת נִדְמֽוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Man that is in honour, and understandeth not, is like the beasts that perish.
verses.rendering:
Humanity in its splendor, without understanding,
is like the beasts that perish.
verses.expanded_rendering: The refrain returns with one crucial change. In verse 13: bal yalin ('will not endure, will not lodge'). Here: ve-lo yavin ('and does not understand'). The shift from durability to comprehension deepens the indictment. It is not merely that wealth cannot make you permanent — it cannot even make you wise. adam biqar ve-lo yavin ('humanity in its preciousness and not understanding') — you can have all the splendor in the world and still lack the basic insight that it will not save you. nimshal ka-behemot nidmu ('like the beasts they perish') — the animal comparison returns. Without understanding, the difference between a wealthy human and a fattened ox is nil. Both eat well, both die, and neither comprehends what is happening to them. The psalm closes where it began: with the call for universal hearing (v. 2) and the observation that most do not listen.
translator_notes: The two forms of the refrain together deliver the psalm's complete message. Verse 13: wealth does not make you last. Verse 21: wealth does not make you wise. The only thing that defeats death is God's ransom (v. 16), and the only thing that brings understanding is wisdom (vv. 4-5) — both are divine gifts, not human achievements.
